what_it_does: A design note that identifies two mobile gesture pitfalls for a long-press drag-to-change-level interaction and recommends making tap the primary selection affordance while reserving long-press for a secondary power menu.
observations: Identifies a selection-vs-scroll war on mobile Safari for long-press gestures inside annotatable surfaces.
Recommends calling preventDefault on touchstart/selectstart only inside the annotatable surface and deliberately toggling -webkit-user-select and -webkit-touch-callout.
Notes that vertical drag-to-change-level reuses the page scroll axis and therefore requires an unambiguous, haptic-confirmed latch.
Calls for a live outline preview of what will commit on release and per-level haptic ticks as non-negotiable.
States that MSG2 reframes the gesture: normal tap selects at the document's natural block level and long-press opens an expert power menu.
References real-device verification on Tim's actual device and cites Medium, Hypothesis, and Apple's lift pattern.
standouts: The requirement for haptic-confirmed latch plus live outline preview is labeled non-negotiable.
Long-press is repositioned as a right-click-style power menu rather than the main selection gesture.
The note explicitly flags real-device-only verification for iOS gesture behavior.
conventions: Uses YAML frontmatter with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, and history fields.
Uses markdown section heading and bullet list for design rationale.
Links to parent item via kind: part_of in frontmatter.
Uses bold emphasis and named references (Tim, BD-I, MSG2) consistent with informal channel notes.
concerns: No code or implementation details are present; advice is design-level and platform-specific.
References to MSG2, Tim, BD-I, and P6 are undefined within the file.
Claims about iOS Vibration API support and haptic behavior are presented as needing verification.
Recommendations assume a particular document/block-level selection model that is not described here.
notes: A mobile-UX cautionary note arguing that the long-press level selector must use tap-to-select as the default and reserve long-press for an expert power menu backed by haptics and a live preview.
questions: What product or feature is the 'level selector' part of?
Who or what is MSG2, Tim, BD-I, and P6?
What is the existing tap-to-select behavior being referenced?
What is the parent item board://item-64466290?
Which platforms besides iOS Safari need to be supported?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated title and section match the content: it is exactly a design note about granularity, the long-press gesture, and mobile pitfalls.
apparent_themes: Mobile gesture conflict with native OS behaviors
intention_signals: To prevent a mobile gesture design from shipping with scroll/callout conflicts and ambiguous mode changes by steering the team toward a tap-primary, long-press-secondary pattern with strong sensory feedback.
novelty: It explicitly reframes long-press as a desktop-right-click-style 'power menu' for secondary actions rather than the main selection gesture, and treats haptic preview as a non-negotiable requirement.
contribution: It contributes the specific mobile gesture failure modes (selection-vs-scroll war, vertical drag vs. scroll collision) and a concrete UX pattern (tap-to-select, haptic-confirmed long-press latch, live outline preview, long-press power menu) as design constraints for a fused system to preserve.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard design note item that warns about mobile Safari long-press pitfalls for a drag-to-change-level selector and recommends tap-to-select as the primary affordance with long-press reserved for a haptic-backed expert power menu.
intention_for_embedding: To record mobile gesture design constraints and steer the implementation away from scroll-conflicting long-press selection toward a tap-primary, long-press-secondary interaction model.
